title: W&B Models end-to-end demo
transcript: [music] Hey, my name is Anu and I'm a part of the Weights and Biases by Coreview team. I'm here to talk to you about Weights and Biases models, a platform designed to be a system of record for all your model training. With just a few lines of code, your team is able to track your end toend ML development cycle. From all the metrics from pre-training to valuation, any artifacts and media generated in the process, all gets tracked within weights and biases. And we give you tools like workspaces that are customizable to reports you can generate with them in order to speed up debugging and collaboration across these projects. Weights and biases as a platform makes sure that your team, the MLES and data scientists and MLOps engineers can focus on data prep, modeling and fine-tuning while weights and biases does the heavy lifting on tracking all of those metrics, tracking model and data set lineages and tracking every parameter that goes into your training exercises to ensure very good reproducibility and auditability of these projects. We'll also discuss how these assets can be linked to registries to make them available across your organization and automations that will help your team automate repetitive tasks like report writing, testing, and deployment of these models. Let's get started. Here is my workspace where I am training a series of reinforcement learning models using the Isaac Labs framework. Now these experiments are running on a core viv L40 GPU cluster but you can easily run weights and biases on any hardware within a public cloud environment or something that's hosted locally for your team. Now you can also use any ML library or framework that makes sense for your projects. For all of those libraries from Keras to PyTorch to hugging face transformers and everything else out there, weights and biases will still be able to track things like GPU utilization, allocated memory, so your team can correctly understand how you are using the hardware for these projects that you're working on. But most importantly, weights and biases will be able to track those actual training metrics and loss curves that help your team understand and profile how the training process is going for these experiments and models. For any metrics that are sent to weights and biases, a visualiz visualization like this is automatically created. So your team does not have to go in and manually set these charts up, which for our team saves us a lot of time. There's also the ability to modify these charts and customize them to your need. At a local level, these modifications can exist at a specific chart like adding smoothing to a specific metric. Let's say running average with a high number. So my team can see the trajectory of the rewards and its profile instead of looking at the noisy underlying data. I can apply similar customizations at an entire panel level by doing the same settings down here in the smoothing part or even at a whole workspace level, which we'll see in a few minutes. One of the things that my team appreciates the most is the ability to set an alert on any of these metrics that are automatically tracked within Weights and Biases. Just by clicking this alert icon right here, your team is able to define a condition. let's say a 50% stepover overstep jump and decide exactly who should receive that as a notification an entire channel somebody specific in your team I'll select the channel now every time there is a 50% jump somebody in my team will get an alert so they can go investigate why is that happening with the training but these metrics aren't just what Brits and vices tracks we also track media associated with each of these experiments so your team can understand how your models are performing at each of these steps by getting this visual feedback. In my case, these videos are simulations of the actual training taking place on my hardware. And I can jump through different steps using the step slider to see exactly how my models behave early on in the training when they're not very good and when I jump to a later stage in the training when they're able to accomplish the task, getting that green table signal. This visual feedback and tying it back to the metrics is something my team really appreciates because it helps us understand not just from the point of view of the metrics but also the tasks how my models are performing. The steps syncing slider applies to other types of training as well where you might be doing generations image video or media or text. So you can see how your model is performing at each of those steps. Now, if your team is doing some kind of hyperparameter tuning and exploring a large space of metrics, weights and biases has an entity called sweeps that helps you organize these experiments. In my case, I'm exploring mostly the amount of training or number of epics I'm training over. And I can very quickly understand how different starting conditions and parameters result in a different set of outcomes for my projects in terms of the cumulative reward because that's the key metric I'm tracking. You'll see all these charts within weights and biases are synced. So when I introduce more experiments in here, something like my EBAL tab where I'm logging results post training by running actual real world evaluations also gets updated. We'll talk about how these evaluations can be automated as well. But what teams using weights and biases like mine really appreciate is the fact that you can have the evaluation and post-training results mapped right in the same workspace as the training and training metrics which allows our team to very quickly go from the realworld evaluation to the simulations that we track within Weights and Biases all the way back to the specific experiments we're running. really helping close the loop on that end toend training view of any project within our environment. So far, we've looked at the default workspace that is generated for your team out of the box by weights and biases. But you can easily create custom views for each user within your project to make sure they can organize it relevant to their needs and their contributions to the project. You can even have saved views that are shared across users within this organization. For example, the semantic colors workspace that I have here. Instead of using a already existing color palette, I'm able to select keybased colors, allowing me to define how each experiment is colored based on these reward metrics that exist within my environment. This helps my team have a better visual understanding of which experiments achieve the best results with higher cumulative rewards and which experiments don't or take a really long time to do so. Now jumping back to our original workspace, you'll notice for every run, weights and biases is capturing a lot of information. The state of the run, specific tasks you have, all the way down to all the parameters passed within each and every run. You can definitely go ahead and filter them right here to understand exactly how a specific set of experiments, let's say in my case, all the experiments with the lift task are performing. And you can also click into a specific run to understand how that experiment rolls out over time. Now you can see here I can look at the system metrics for that specific run, the really nice smooth rewards chart. So I can see the trajectory of the recover accumulation. But more importantly within this overview tab of this run, I can see all the conditions that this experiment started with. how long it took, which Git repository and commit was used to start this experiment, even which specific script was used, and all the parameters that are passed in at training time. So even if there's a diff between your git command and the experiment you ran, you know, for auditability and reproducibility purposes, you will have exactly what was passed in during training time. And similarly all the output and summary metrics that were emitted during the experiment and training. Now B beyond these conditions we also track logs files that are coming out so your team knows the exact config requirements packages that were used including the actual script used to run the experiment. But I think most important and valuable thing that we consider weights and biases tracks for our team is these artifacts. So we know exactly what is used as an input artifact for the training and what output artifacts are generated. This can be the videos and media but more importantly the models that are generated as the outcome of your training. You can view and model and weights and biases will automatically track which version it is, which latest model it is. You can introduce tags to track them as production models. And we'll come back to this test tag in a little bit. But you'll see behind the scenes, we'll give you instructions on how to exactly use this artifact within any training or evaluations in the future and also track where these models have been checked out for which activities. And what teams really like is this lineage view that helps them understand which input artifacts created by who result in this output artifact that we're currently looking at and where that artifact has been consumed. like this add eval panels run where I actually ran an evaluation on this model and locked the results back within weights and biases. Now these artifacts are local to your project. Only people with access to the project can view them. But we offer you the ability to connect or link these artifacts to a registry that can be an orwide entity. So the artifacts can be shared with a larger set of collaborators, project managers, de uh deployment teams, mlops, etc. Let's look at these registries in a little bit more detail. Here I have the Isaac lab dex suit RL registry which contains all reinforcement learning models trained by my team that are qualified or good enough to be passed to production. You see, they all have this test tag, which we will investigate in a few minutes, that helps me run evaluations to make sure these match the quality that we're looking for. And these protected aliases that can only be managed by registry admins. Registries by themselves have independent sharing abilities to the projects and the artifacts. So you can set permissions to make sure the right people have access to go in and edit them as admins, introduce these protected aliases or uh give access to a specific set of users like my SMLE or that can go and check these models out but cannot modify them. This complex access control allows you to set up access that is relevant to your organization's needs. You can build these registries with data sets and other kinds of artifacts as well, not just models. So you can have a predefined policy like my team uses this initial policy for a lot of our training exercises or specific data sets and make sure your team knows exactly what kind of files information can contain within those data sets by introducing a lot of metadata and metrics around each of these objects within your environment. Now one thing common between projects and registries is automations. Here I have a special automation defined for my models registry which is called the ensemble eval hardware testing. This can only be run by a specific user that is an admin to this registry giving the admins a lot of control here. What this does is it deploys my model with other set of models that we're testing onto real hardware which can be expensive. Thus the reason to gate keep it at an admin level. How this automations runs and a lot of the automations in weights and bices do is by using a web hook. So registry level automations allow you to trigger actions when an new version is linked to the registry or a new alias is added. example, my hardware testing alias that is only available on this model. That means it's the only one tested on hardware that my team should check out. But at a project level, you can have automations in a lot of different shapes. We saw some of them earlier as notifications going to Slack. of the Slack notification type. But we also have these web hook based automations that your team can use like this ensemble eval where I do something very similar to the hardware eval but at a project level in a simulated environment. What this does is every time that test tag that we've been looking at is added to any artifact in this environment, this payload is passed to GitHub actions. In your case, it can be a CI/CD tool that your team uses to build model pipelines and it triggers an eval and logs those evaluation metrics you saw earlier back into my workspace. My team does not need to slack someone or tell someone that hey, we need to run this eval. This already set up within this environment to use. You could have a lot of different triggers based on a run or experiment metric condition or the tags and aliases that we talked about earlier to set off these automations and kick off a web hook based workflow. One of my favorite ones here is this report generation. This one looks for a finish status on an experiment to automatically generate a report which looks like this for some of my experiments. This report uses a Python script to automatically pin the right media and charts that are specified in my script including those evaluation metrics in order to create an autogenerated report for every experiment that I run. You can easily define these reports manually uh in markdown format right within our project and document why certain metrics are good versus the uh bad versus the outcomes that you're seeing within your projects. Or you can use our SDK to automatically generate this report and even entire workspaces using our SDK. Putting all of this together, weights and biases becomes the central hub for your team to track projects, metrics, all the media generated within it and do the mlop's work of organizing and triggering different workflows to help your teams focus on the algorithm building while weights and biases does all the tracking and logistics that support your ML and AI teams. If you want to try this, just go to wanb wb.ai and try this for yourself today. [music]
